questions.question: What is the difference between interface and type in TypeScript?
questions.command: aura -m deepseek/deepseek-chat --auto --readonly --max-turns 1 What is the difference between interface and type in TypeScript?

I verify." ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── Task: What is the difference between interface and type in TypeScript? ◆ thinking… ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── Ruby Principle Only 2 prior attempt(s) for this pattern (minimum 3) — giving Ruby a chance to learn. ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── ✓ Trying Ruby (granite4.1:3b)… ◆ thinking… ◆ Context: ░░░░░░░░░░░┊░░┊░░┊░░ 2% (2.8k/128k) · compact @ 55% (70k) · 1 turns **Interface vs. Type in TypeScript** | Aspect | **Interface** | **Type (type alias)** | |--------|----------------|-----------------------| | **Purpose / Intent** | Designed for *declaration* – it describes a shape that can be extended, implemented, or used as a contract (`implements`). It’s the canonical way to define object shapes in TypeScript. | A *named type* that aliases an existing type (or a combination of types) without adding any extra members beyond what the aliased type already has. Useful for brevity, reuse, and when you need to combine multiple types or constraints (`&`, `|`). | | **Extensibility** | Can be extended with `extends` clause or implemented by classes/objects (e.g., `interface Animal { name: string } interface Dog extends Animal { breed: string }`). | Cannot extend other types directly; you can only alias a combination of existing types (`type Point = { x: number; y: number }; type Rect = { top: number; left: number; width: number; height: number; point: Point }`). | | **Immutability** | Interfaces are *mutable* – you can add new members to an interface without breaking compatibility (unless you change a member’s name or signature). | Type aliases are *immutable* once defined – adding a property will cause a TypeScript error because the type definition changes. | | **Naming Convention** | Usually descriptive nouns (`User`, `Request`, `Response`). Often used for public APIs and class contracts. | Can be any valid identifier; often prefixed with `t` or `type` to indicate it’s a generic alias (`Point`, `Rect`, `MaybeString`). | | **Usage Scenarios** | • Defining classes, objects, function signatures.
• Declaring contract for third‑party libraries.
• Using in `implements` clauses. | • Combining multiple constraints (e.g., `ReadonlyArray` = `readonly Array`).
• Shortening long type expressions (`type ID = string`).
• When you need to reuse a complex union or intersection without re‑typing it everywhere. | | **Example** | ```ts\ninterface Pet {\n name: string;\n sound(): string;\n}\n\nclass Dog implements Pet {\n name: string;\n constructor(name) { this.name = name; }\n sound() { return 'Woof!'; }\n}\n``` | ```ts\ntype Animal = { age: number; species: string };\ntype Pet = Animal & { sound(): string };\nclass Dog implements Pet {\n // inherits `age` and `species` from Animal, plus `sound`\n}\n``` | | **Compatibility** | Adding a new property to an interface does not break existing code that satisfies the original interface. | Changing or removing a member in a type alias will cause TypeScript errors because the underlying type definition changes. | | **When to Prefer** | • When you need to define a contract for classes, objects, or functions.
• Want to extend/implement other interfaces (`extends`, `implements`). | • Need to combine existing types (union/intersection) without re‑defining them everywhere.
• Want a concise alias for complex type expressions. | **Bottom line:** - **Interfaces** are the go‑to tool for defining *shapes* that can be extended or implemented, making them ideal for public APIs and class contracts. - **Type aliases** (the `type` keyword) are used when you want to create a reusable shorthand for existing types—especially useful for combining multiple constraints or creating complex unions/intersections without altering the original type definitions. ✓ Ruby handled the task without escalation. ⚠ Ruby Principle: enough failures accumulated — project is ready for fine-tuning. ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── ✓ Done 1 turn · 0 tool call **Interface vs. Type in TypeScript** | Aspect | **Interface** | **Type (type alias)** | |--------|----------------|-----------------------| | **Purpose / Intent** | Designed for *declaration* – it describes a shape that can be extended, implemented, or used as a contract (`implements`). It’s the canonical way to define object shapes in TypeScript. | A *named type* that aliases an existing type (or a combination of types) without adding any extra members beyond what the aliased type already has. Useful for brevity, reuse, and when you need to combine multiple types or constraints (`&`, `|`). | | **Extensibility** | Can be extended with `extends` clause or implemented by classes/objects (e.g., `interface Animal { name: string } interface Dog extends Animal { breed: string }`). | Cannot extend other types directly; you can only alias a combination of existing types (`type Point = { x: number; y: number }; type Rect = { top: number; left: number; width: number; height: number; point: Point }`). | | **Immutability** | Interfaces are *mutable* – you can add new members to an interface without breaking compatibility (unless you change a member’s name or signature). | Type aliases are *immutable* once defined – adding a property will cause a TypeScript error because the type definition changes. | | **Naming Convention** | Usually descriptive nouns (`User`, `Request`, `Response`). Often used for public APIs and class contracts. | Can be any valid identifier; often prefixed with `t` or `type` to indicate it’s a generic alias (`Point`, `Rect`, `MaybeString`). | | **Usage Scenarios** | • Defining classes, objects, function signatures.
• Declaring contract for third‑party libraries.
• Using in `implements` clauses. | • Combining multiple constraints (e.g., `ReadonlyArray` = `readonly Array`).
• Shortening long type expressions (`type ID = string`).
• When you need to reuse a complex union or intersection without re‑typing it everywhere. | | **Example** | ```ts\ninterface Pet {\n name: string;\n sound(): string;\n}\n\nclass Dog implements Pet {\n name: string;\n constructor(name) { this.name = name; }\n sound() { return 'Woof!'; }\n}\n``` | ```ts\ntype Animal = { age: number; species: string };\ntype Pet = Animal & { sound(): string };\nclass Dog implements Pet {\n // inherits `age` and `species` from Animal, plus `sound`\n}\n``` | | **Compatibility** | Adding a new property to an interface does not break existing code that satisfies the original interface. | Changing or removing a member in a type alias will cause TypeScript errors because the underlying type definition changes. | | **When to Prefer** | • When you need to define a contract for classes, objects, or functions.
• Want to extend/implement other interfaces (`extends`, `implements`). | • Need to combine existing types (union/intersection) without re‑defining them everywhere.
• Want a concise alias for complex type expressions. | **Bottom line:** - **Interfaces** are the go‑to tool for defining *shapes* that can be extended or implemented, making them ideal for public APIs and class contracts. - **Type aliases** (the `type` keyword) are used when you want to create a reusable shorthand for existing types—especially useful for combining multiple constraints or creating complex unions/intersections without altering the original type definitions. ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── ↳ 4,234 tokens (3,457 in / 777 out) · est. $0.0000
